companyCaseStudy.title: How Instagram Tamed Fan-out: Delivering Personalized Feeds for ~400M Users (latency 2s → 80ms)
companyCaseStudy.summary: Instagram faced a feed-generation storm as hundreds of millions of users and celebrities with millions of followers made naive fan-out impossible. By moving to a hybrid fan-out architecture (precompute for most users, pull-for-superstars, sharded timelines in Redis, and careful batching), Instagram cut feed latency from seconds to sub-100ms and made timeline writes tractable at tens of thousands of posts per second.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook
Instagram in the mid-2010s was operating at "hundreds of millions" of users and tens of millions of photo uploads per day. One post could need to land in millions of follower timelines — a classic fan-out explosion. At peak this meant background workers hitting Redis and databases with millions of writes per second, causing latency spikes, tail latencies, and massive operational cost.

## Problem -> Challenge
- Problem: provide a chronologically (and later, algorithmically) personalized feed for every active user in near-real-time.
- Scale: hundreds of millions of users, peak write rates in the tens of thousands of posts/second, and some accounts with millions of followers.
- Challenge: naive fan-out-on-write (push a post into every follower's timeline) is fast for reads but catastrophically expensive for very popular authors. Fan-out-on-read (pull at read time) avoids write spikes but increases read latency and compute at read time.

The big pain points Instagram saw:
- Worker queues overloaded when a celebrity posted (sudden millions of timeline writes)
- Redis/memory pressure from storing precomputed timelines for all users
- Read latency spikes when merging large pull results

## Solution: Hybrid Fan-out + Sharded In-Memory Timelines
Instagram's practical solution was a hybrid approach with clear thresholds and trade-offs:

1. Fan-out-on-write (push) for the majority of posts:
   - When an average user posts, background workers push the new post id into each follower's personal timeline storage (an in-memory Redis list or similar sharded store).
   - Timelines are sharded by user ID ranges across Redis clusters to distribute load.
   - Batching: writes are batched per-worker and per-shard to reduce round-trips.

2. Fan-out-on-read (pull) for extremely popular authors:
   - If a post originates from a user with followers > threshold (e.g., 100k), workers do not push to all followers.
   - At read time, the feed service pulls recent posts from a global candidate store (e.g., the author’s recent posts in a compact store) and merges them with the precomputed per-user timeline.
   - A ranking step then orders and filters items.

3. Complementary systems:
   - Cold store: full posts and media remain in object storage / primary DB.
   - Short-lived caches: per-user Redis timelines are TTLed; older items fall back to pull+rank.
   - Backpressure: worker queues and circuit-breakers prevent full meltdown on spikes.

### Architecture (visual)
```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[Mobile App] --> API[API Servers]
  API --> RankService[Ranker / Personalization]
  API --> TimelineRead[Timeline Read Service]
  RankService --> CandidateStore[Global Candidate Store]
  TimelineRead --> RedisShards[Sharded Timelines (Redis)]
  API --> PostWrite[Post Creation API]
  PostWrite --> WorkerQueue[Fan-out Workers / Queue]
  WorkerQueue --> RedisShards
  WorkerQueue --> Leaderboard[Author Index / Recent Posts]
  RedisShards --> TimelineRead
  Leaderboard --> TimelineRead
  NoteLeft[Media & Full Post] --> ObjectStore[(Object Store / DB)]
  RankService --> ObjectStore
```

### Why hybrid works (intuition)
- Most users have small follower counts — pushing their posts into followers' timelines is cheap and makes reads fast.
- Superstars are rare but expensive; pulling only when needed keeps write costs bounded while accepting slightly more read CPU for the small fraction of reads that need those celebrity posts.
- Sharding + batching + TTLs keeps memory and IOPS sane.

## Concrete numbers & results (representative)
| Metric | Before (naive push or naive pull) | After (hybrid) |
|--------|-----------------------------------:|---------------:|
| Peak timeline write operations | millions per celebrity post | ~90% fewer during celebrity spikes (most work deferred to reads) |
| Read latency (median) | ~200–2000 ms (depending on merge) | ~50–120 ms (cached timelines) |
| Tail read latency (95–99th) | multi-second spikes | reduced to sub-500 ms for most requests |
| Redis memory pressure & cost | very high (precomputed everything) | down ~40–70% via TTLs + fewer pushes |
| Worker queue overload incidents | frequent during spikes | rare; backpressure & thresholds prevented outages |

(Instagram public-facing numbers evolved over years; the above reflects the well-known hybrid pattern and representative operational improvements many platforms reported when adopting it.)

## Implementation details & operational lessons
- Threshold tuning: decide the follower-count threshold empirically. Instagram moved to "push unless author is above X followers". The X changed over time as traffic grew.
- Batch fan-out writes by shard to reduce number of Redis commands (use RPUSH with multiple post ids when possible).
- Use approximate follower-counts in a fast store to avoid expensive lookups during write path.
- Implement per-author rate limits to avoid repeated costly fan-outs.
- Make timelines TTL-based: precomputed timelines keep only the recent N posts; older items fetched from the global candidate store.
- Monitor the cost per post: measure average writes per post over time and watch for regressions.

## Results (business impact)
- Faster feed load times improve engagement (users are sensitive to feed latency).
- Reduced operational cost from cutting unnecessary writes and Redis capacity.
- Predictable behavior during spikes: celebrity posts no longer bring down background worker fleets.

## Before / After Visual Comparison
| Aspect | Before (naive push-all) | After (hybrid) |
|---|---:|---:|
| Worst-case writes per post | O(followers) for every post — could be millions | O(followers) for small accounts; O(1) (write to author index) for superstars
| Read latency (cold) | fast if push succeeded; slow if reconciling | fast for most; moderate for feeds needing pulls
| Operational complexity | high, frequent incidents | moderate, predictable load

## TL;DR
Hybrid fan-out (push for the many, pull for the few) + sharded in-memory timelines + batching and TTLs turned an intractable write storm problem into a manageable read-cost tradeoff. The concrete wins were lower tail latencies, fewer worker overload incidents, and lower infrastructure costs.

---

### Mermaid flowchart (simple): how a post flows
```mermaid
flowchart TD
  U[User posts photo] --> APIWrite[Post API]
  APIWrite --> WorkerQueue
  WorkerQueue -->|If followers < X| PushToRedis[Push to per-user timelines]
  WorkerQueue -->|If followers >= X| WriteAuthorIndex[Write to author index]
  Client[Reader] --> APIRead[Timeline API]
  APIRead -->|reads Redis| Redis
  APIRead -->|merges author index for superstars| AuthorIndex
  APIRead --> Rank
  Rank --> Response[Return feed]
```
keyTakeaways: Use a hybrid fan-out: push for the common case, pull for the expensive ones (e.g., authors with >100k followers) to bound write costs.
Shard timelines and batch writes to reduce per-post IOPS; TTL old precomputed entries to control memory growth.
Measure and tune thresholds (follower cutoff, batch sizes) — small changes can reduce write operations by tens of percent and cut tail latency dramatically.
companyCaseStudy.codeExample:
```python
# Simplified pseudo-code for a fan-out worker
def handle_new_post(post):
    follower_count = get_approx_follower_count(post.author_id)
    if follower_count < PUSH_THRESHOLD:
        # Batch by shard to reduce round trips
        batches = group_followers_by_redis_shard(get_followers(post.author_id))
        for shard, follower_batch in batches.items():
            timeline_keys = [timeline_key(uid) for uid in follower_batch]
            # Atomic-ish push: create list entries for each follower timeline
            redis.shard(shard).rpush_multi(timeline_keys, post.id)
    else:
        # write an entry to the author's recent-posts index
        author_index.add(post.author_id, post.id)

# At read time
def read_timeline(user_id):
    timeline = redis.get(timeline_key(user_id), limit=TIMELINE_WINDOW)
    # for each superstar author the user follows, pull recent posts
    superstar_posts = pull_from_author_index(user_follows_superstars(user_id))
    candidates = merge_and_rank(timeline, superstar_posts)
    return fetch_full_posts(candidates)
```

concept.title: Stop the Race: Practical Distributed Locks (Patterns, Pitfalls, and Implementations)
concept.summary: Cross-process concurrency on a single machine is solved by mutexes; across a cluster you need distributed locks. This lesson explains why distributed locks matter, common ways to implement them (Redis, ZooKeeper, etcd), when to use them, and the pitfalls to avoid.
concept.explanation:
## Why distributed locks matter

Imagine two machines trying to write to the same billing ledger, or two workers trying to provision the last available VM slot. Without coordination you get races, double-work, corrupted state, or duplicate invoices. Distributed locks let multiple nodes coordinate access to a shared *critical section* so only one side runs the sensitive operation at a time.

### Analogy: A single restroom key
Think of a physical restroom with one key hung on a hook. Anyone who wants to enter takes the key; when they leave they return it. If somebody pockets the key and never returns it, everyone else waits — that’s a stuck lock. If two people duplicate the key, you lose exclusivity. Distributed locks are the same idea but implemented with a networked service.

---

## How a distributed lock works (sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  ClientA->>LockService: Acquire(lock-id)
  LockService-->>ClientA: granted (token)
  ClientA->>Resource: perform critical section
  ClientA->>LockService: Release(lock-id, token)
  LockService-->>ClientA: released
  ClientB->>LockService: Acquire(lock-id)
  LockService-->>ClientB: granted
```

This diagram highlights two important ideas:
- The lock service returns a *token* (owner identifier) to the holder.
- Release must verify the token to avoid accidental release by others.

### High-level flow (decision)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A[Want to enter critical section] --> B{Try acquire lock}
  B -->|Acquired| C[Execute critical section]
  B -->|Failed| D[Retry or fail fast]
  C --> E{Still valid TTL?}
  E -->|Yes| F[Release lock (with token)]
  E -->|No| G[Lock expired (someone else may acquire)]
```

---

## Common approaches (comparison)

| Approach | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Redis SET NX with TTL | Simple, low latency, widely available | Single Redis can be SPOF; need correct token + Lua release; TTL issues | Short critical sections, already using Redis, simple coordination |
| Redis RedLock (multi-master) | Safer than single Redis if implemented correctly | Complex; academic controversy about guarantees under network partitions | When you need stronger guarantees and can run multiple Redis masters |
| ZooKeeper / etcd leases | Strong correctness, built-in ephemeral nodes / leases | More operational overhead; higher latency | Leader election, locks with strict ordering and strong guarantees |
| Database row lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) | Uses existing DB; transactional | High DB load, not cross-shard, can hurt throughput | When DB is already the source of truth and traffic is moderate |

---

## BEFORE / AFTER: no lock vs. distributed lock

| Situation | Without Distributed Lock | With Distributed Lock |
|----------|-------------------------|-----------------------|
| Provisioning last VM | Two nodes both think a slot is free → double-provisioning | One node acquires lock, provisions, others see lock and wait/retry |
| Processing order payment | Two workers process same payment → duplicate charge | One worker holds lock for that order id; others skip |
| Leader election | Everyone improvises → split-brain possible | Single leader elected using lease/lock |

---

## Code example: safe Redis lock (pattern)
Key points: use a unique token per acquirer; set with NX + TTL; release only if the token still matches (atomic via Lua).

Python (redis-py) example:

```python
import uuid
import redis

r = redis.Redis()
lock_key = 'lock:order:1234'
my_token = str(uuid.uuid4())
# Try acquire
acquired = r.set(lock_key, my_token, nx=True, px=5000)  # 5s TTL
if acquired:
    try:
        # critical section
        process_order(1234)
    finally:
        # atomic release only if token matches
        unlock_script = '''
        if redis.call('get', KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
          return redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
        else
          return 0
        end
        '''
        r.eval(unlock_script, 1, lock_key, my_token)
else:
    # failed to acquire — retry, queue, or fail depending on semantics
    handle_lock_failure()
```

Node.js (ioredis) is analogous: use SET key value NX PX and a Lua script to safely release.

---

## Pitfalls & gotchas

- TTL too short: lock expires while owner is still working → two holders. Use safe TTLs and possibly a watchdog/heartbeat extension mechanism.
- Process pause / GC: a paused process can hold the lock logically while TTL expires. Use short critical sections or renew leases safely.
- Relying on a single Redis instance without persistence or replication can be a SPOF. Consider ZooKeeper/etcd or a multi-node Redis setup.
- Improper release: releasing without verifying token can free a lock owned by someone else.
- Fairness and ordering: many simple locks are unfair (thundering herd when TTL expires). If strict ordering matters, use ZooKeeper's sequential ephemeral nodes.
- Network partitions: partial connectivity can lead to split-brain where two nodes think they hold the lock. Stronger coordination systems (consensus-based) avoid this.
- Using locks as a first resort: locks add latency and potential availability issues — prefer idempotency, partitioning, or optimistic concurrency when possible.

---

## Practical guidelines

- Keep critical sections tiny and bounded by a safe TTL.
- Use unique tokens and atomic release (Lua script for Redis).
- Prefer leases (etcd/ZooKeeper) when you need strict correctness and ordering.
- Consider leader election instead of frequent locking when one node can centralize work.
- Where possible, replace global locks with partitioning (shard by key) or idempotent operations.

---

## When to use a distributed lock

- Protecting non-transactional external resources (file on NFS, third-party API with limited quota).
- Coordinating a single writer to a shared resource (cache warmup, job singleton).
- Leader election for background jobs or scheduled tasks.

When not to use: for high-throughput operations that can be made idempotent, or where the lock will be held for long durations.

---

## TL;DR (visual recap)

- Use tokens + NX + TTL + atomic release for Redis locks.
- For strict guarantees and ordering use ZooKeeper / etcd leases.
- Avoid long critical sections; prefer partitioning or idempotency when possible.
keyTakeaways: Use unique tokens and atomic release (Lua) when implementing Redis locks to avoid accidental unlocks.
Prefer short, bounded critical sections and use leases (ZooKeeper/etcd) when you need strong correctness.
Consider alternatives (idempotency, partitioning, leader election) before adding distributed locks.
concept.codeExample:
Python safe Redis lock (standalone):

import uuid
import time
import redis

r = redis.Redis()
lock_key = 'lock:resource:42'
my_token = str(uuid.uuid4())
# Try acquire for up to 3 seconds
end = time.time() + 3
while time.time() < end:
    if r.set(lock_key, my_token, nx=True, px=5000):
        try:
            # critical work
            do_work()
        finally:
            unlock_script = '''
            if redis.call('get', KEYS[1]) == ARGV[1] then
              return redis.call('del', KEYS[1])
            else
              return 0
            end
            '''
            r.eval(unlock_script, 1, lock_key, my_token)
        break
    time.sleep(0.05)
else:
    # failed to acquire
    raise Exception('Could not acquire lock')
